w:r.w:t: Chapter 1:
Stardust to Stardust

A torchlight clenched in the boy's hand flickered, casting suspicious shadows around the four-room house as he tiptoed down the dimly lit hallway. The flame danced nervously, a tiny, unreliable beacon in the oppressive gloom. His heart hammered against his ribs, a frantic drumbeat in the suffocating stillness. Each step on the cool sunbaked stone threatened to betray him—any clank or clatter could undo his desperate mission. He had to reach his father. He had to try, just one more time, before the point of no return.

The hallway had become a labyrinth, a testament to his father's unraveling. Piles of pottery shards, crusted with dried filth and the ghostly remnants of meals, loomed like ancient cairns, treacherous underfoot, whispering of forgotten feasts and shattered lives. Rotting textiles, teeming with maggots, draped over discarded animal bones. The air itself felt thick, not merely with dust, but with the cloying scent of stagnation, clinging to the back of his throat. Withered leather sandals lay scattered, their straps brittle and cracked; rusted bronze tools, long abandoned, added to the metallic tang of decay; and desiccated, shriveled fruits, once vibrant, now clung to their seeds like mummified offerings. The stench—desiccated herbs mixed with excrement—stung Dan's nostrils, a vile perfume that spoke of surrender, of a life slowly consuming itself from the inside out. Swarms of flies, their buzzing a morbid symphony, rose from mounds of decaying food, swirling in living clouds over the corruption. This was a place where everything good had gone to die, and the remnants were left to fester.

The boy, with messy hair and kind eyes, picked his way through the refuse. His bare feet, usually calloused and firm, now felt as though they touched a fresh grave with every silent step. A shiver pierced him. It wasn't just the filth; it was the wrongness of it all, a palpable sense of something deeply disturbed. His father, Aviel, once a respected scholar whose wisdom had been sought by many, now saw treasure in rot, abundance in decay. He had traded insight for squalor, a mind once bright now clouded by the accumulating detritus.
Dan's breath caught in his throat, a sharp, metallic tang on his tongue. This house was a slow drowning. They were not living here; they were sinking, slowly, from the inside out.

His bare feet found a small, deliberate construction amidst the chaos—a fresh altar, a focal point for his father's twisted devotion. It was a sad irony that mocked the very concept of plenty. Aviel had fled from Philistia, but he brought their gods with him to Hebron, and after his mother's death, Dagon became his only source of hope. Around this crude shrine, Dan saw stones and pebbles, gathered with obsessive care; bones arranged like abandoned offerings; clay tablets whispering forgotten stories, their cuneiform script half-erased by time and neglect. Each element placed with perverse reverence, a mockery of true worship. His father was building something, yes, but building it from ruin.

The name Dagon almost came from him unbidden, a bitter taste on the back of his tongue, like stale ash. That damned god had stolen his father's soul. And Time—that insatiable, devouring force, a silent, relentless predator—bore the brunt of guilt. Time was not just the passage of moments but something else entirely, a force Dan would later understand had a name whispered in shadows. He knew, with the certainty of youth and the wisdom of suffering, that he would meet them both if he continued on this path—the path of stagnation, the path of decay. But this boy had a different path calling to him, a path whispered by the wind and traced by the stars.

Outside, stars—ancient and indifferent—and a full moon on its third day watched over Hebron. The city, nestled in the Judean Hills, was a fortress of walls and vineyards. The air hummed with the scent of sun-baked earth and distant woodsmoke, while the faint shimmer of oil lamps dotted the darkness like fallen stars. A whisper of cool breeze carried the ghost of grapevines and the promise of dawn, stirring the leaves in the surrounding olive groves. King David reigned here and all of Judah, his power rising quickly throughout the lands, a new order blooming. And new orders, you see, rarely arrive without a certain amount of… shuffling. A shifting of old pieces, a breaking of old molds.

Though echoes from the marketplace had ceased long ago, the night wasn't silent. The crickets' urgent chirping pulsed in the darkness, a constant, pulsing rhythm, almost like the quick beat of a frightened heart. The wind sighed through brittle grass like a thousand whispers, carrying the scent of dry earth, and the distant bark of a dog cut through the stillness. Every sound threatened to expose Dan, to drag him back into the quicksand of his father's madness. The smaller the sound, the louder it seems when one is trying to be quiet. A twig snapping, a loose stone rolling – they could seem louder than thunder in the stillness of the night, a cruel trick of the ear.

The boy was called Dan, though a name whose full significance he wouldn't understand until later. He had been waiting for this moment—not because the message he carried was easy to deliver, but because telling it would lift the first of many burdens. A weight had settled on his spirit, heavy as the dust that coated his father's possessions, and he yearned to cast it off.

For seven years, Dan had watched helplessly as Father Aviel lost himself. The house, once filled with the warmth of his mother's presence, had slowly become a tomb, each passing season adding another layer of refuse, another shadow to Aviel's eyes. For seven years, he had navigated the growing darkness, trying to hold onto the light within, trying to remember the man his father used to be. For a boy of sixteen, the decision to leave this infected home was perhaps the hardest he had ever faced, a tearing at the roots of his being. He had to leave before he, too, was consumed. Sometimes, the greatest act of love was to seek your own survival, even if it meant leaving a piece of your heart behind.

Dan paused outside Father Aviel's room, the torch still clutched in his hand, its small flame casting frantic shadows against the doorframe. With a quiet snuff, he extinguished the torch, plunging the immediate area into deeper darkness, relying now only on the faint moonlight filtering through the slit in the hallway wall and the memory of light. The smell of burnt wick lingered, a bitter tang in the stagnant air. He confirmed his father was asleep; deep sleep filled the room, a soft, rhythmic symphony of slow, whistling breaths and the occasional, low rumble of a snore. The ache in Dan's chest was not new, but it had grown heavier in recent days, a cold, dull throb behind his ribs. He remembered his father's words from a heated disagreement about gods and idols: “My son, if you are still looking for a god, then pick up a rock and make it so!”

The question burned in Dan's mind. If gods could be made of rocks, formed by the sheer weight of a man's attention and despair, then where should a searching heart like his truly turn? Dan's eyes often searched upward, as if answers resided there, in the vast, swirling tapestry above. He looked past the stars, harboring a suspicion long before the rest that humans were made from them. From dust to dust, from stardust to stardust, he mused. Dreams had often pulled him upward—climbing impossible stairs, leaping across vast chasms, floating among the constellations as if gravity itself had ceased to exist. Each time, he awoke with a strange longing, a sense of having been home. He looked up now, through the narrow slit, and the stars gazed back, patient and knowing, their ancient light a silent beckoning. That was where he would direct his attention. That was where he would go.

Dan knelt by his Father's cot, becoming one with the cold stone. He felt the ancient chill seep into his bare knees, grounding him, rooting him to the very bedrock of the world. He slowed his breath, silencing the frantic voices in his mind as his mother and nurturer, Zuna, had taught him. "Forget yesterday, forget tomorrow, forget now, just be. Empty the vessel," she would say, her voice echoing in his memory, "before you can fill it with the light."

His mother Zuna's words, whispered from beyond the veil, often guided him. She had been a dreamwalker, both blessed and cursed, for some feared her kind and whispered of witches who consorted with cats and commanded elements. (A curious pairing, those witches and cats, wouldn't you agree? Especially when the cat might have a few secrets of his own, and a tale to tell that stretches longer than a tangled ball of yarn. But that, as they say, is another story). Zuna had spoken of journeys beyond the mortal coil, of realms hidden just behind the waking world, waiting for those with the stillness of spirit to find them. "Find a still place," she'd say, "be like the wind, ever moving yet rooted to the earth."

In that stillness, that profound absence of self, he found the still place. He slipped into the Dreamscape, a realm where spirit reigned and flesh held no dominion. Inside the dreamscape, he found his father in an empty field lit by a shy ethereal light stretching to the horizon in shades of gray and silver. It was a blank canvas, desolate but blooming with potential, as things must have been before Time, before the first truth was spoken.

Aviel stood alone in the center, a solitary figure, dust swirling around him like a shroud, not the coarse dust of the earth, but something finer, eternal, the raw material of creation itself. At Dan's will, a surge of power, primal and thrilling as a lightning strike, flowed through him, waking forgotten currents in his blood, making his spirit hum with a profound, almost terrifying energy. The dust, stirred by an unseen hand, began to dance, then to coil like living smoke, thick as morning mist, a swirling vortex of creation. It coalesced, thickening, darkening, taking on substance with a soft, ethereal sigh of transformation.

First, glittering motes of sand rained down, countless crystalline grains forming from the boundless nothingness, whispering softly as they settled upon the desolate field. Then, with a slow, deliberate groan that seemed to echo from the very heart of the dreamscape, these grains knit together, drawing close, hardening, forming smooth, cool pebbles that clicked softly as they accumulated. In turn, these pebbles fractured and grew, erupting into rough, jagged rocks that hailed down from the blank sky—each one a solid, undeniable testament to his burgeoning will, a tangible manifestation of pure thought made terrifyingly real. A sane man, even in the depths of slumber, would have recoiled from such an unnatural genesis, raining down on him. But Aviel, lost in the deep, treacherous currents of his delusion, and directed by Dan's will, perceived only treasure. He scrabbled at the growing piles, his fingers frantic, his hands shaking, stuffing his pockets and then his garments, his eyes burning with a feverish, desperate greed that distorted his face into a grotesque mask of rapacity.

He crammed the ever-burgeoning weight into his pockets, his robes, until his very form was distended, deformed, a monstrous caricature of a man, burdened by the very wealth he so slavishly craved. The stones piled higher, crushing him, pressing against his chest, squeezing the very breath from his lungs. Aviel's feverish eyes glazed over, his mouth open in a soundless gasp, his desperate grasping fingers finally falling limp. The sheer, suffocating weight silenced him, not with a roar but with a profound, internal crack.

Then, Dan summoned a wind, a sigh from the void that rippled through the formless expanse. It swept across the field, gathering the dust and its creations, swirling them into a vortex of dissolution, carrying everything back to nothingness, to the serene oblivion from which it came. Aviel looked up, and for a fleeting moment, saw a single star pierce the oppressive gray, impossibly bright. It pulsed, a faint, insistent heartbeat against the vast canvas of his dream, a promise of something beyond the crushing weight of his hoard.

Dan poured his intent into that vision, shaping it with every fiber of his being: The son must depart on his journey, and the father must prepare his heart. He held the image of the star, a beacon of hope and change, imprinting it upon his father's sleeping mind. Then, gently, with a deep breath, he released his father's consciousness, letting him drift in the gentle rhythm of sleep. Aviel's mind would remain dreamless for the rest of the night, a silent respite from the chaos.

Weak and drained, Dan rekindled his light with flint and pyrite upon exiting his father's room, then retraced his steps through the defiled hallway. The torchlight guttered, casting his slouched shadow in a grotesque parody. He felt smaller now, the energy of the Dreamscape fading, and borrowed confidence spent, leaving him vulnerable once more to the oppressive reality of the house. Each shadow seemed to watch as he gingerly made his way through the maze on the now freezing stone floor.

Back in his room, the door closed with a soft clack. In stark contrast to the decaying labyrinth outside, Dan's small room, stone-walled with a simple cot and a few carefully arranged possessions, was meticulously tidy, a small corner of order he had managed to carve out from the house’s disarray. One might say it was much like putting one's own house in order before attempting to change the world. He sought oblivion on the cot, a temporary reprieve from the dust and the sorrow. Yet, even as darkness claimed him, the stillness of his room began to ripple, and a chilling sense of dread, cold as the deepest ocean, coiled around his fading awareness. The nightmare, it seemed, had already found its way in.

The sea roared, a maelstrom of chaos, a violent, churning abyss of black and white. Dan's small boat, splintered and fragile, was tossed like a toy, helpless against the colossal fury. Waves like mountains crashed around him, a deafening symphony of destruction that vibrated through his very bones. The sky, a suffocating blanket of black, offered no escape, no glimpse of light or hope. His vision fractured into a kaleidoscope of blue, black, and white—water, darkness, foam, swirling endlessly, threatening to consume him. Then, from the very heart of the storm, from the impossible depths, it rose.

At the Southern edge of the world, where the horizon bled into an endless, turbulent gray, a colossal head emerged from the waves. The face, carved from drowned stone, was ancient and impassive, radiating an inhuman cold. Its unblinking eyes, wide as abysses, blacker than the deepest trench, locked onto Dan's, a gaze that stripped away all pretense and left him naked in his terror. Seaweed, thick and rotting, draped its chin like a hideous beard, and barnacles, sharp and encrusted, clung to its cheeks like jewels of corruption. Dan knew, with a certainty that chilled him to the bone, the purpose of this visitation. He had angered a god.

In the far north, where the sea turned to ink, a monstrous tail breached the surface, a colossal fin that dwarfed his shattered boat. Scaled and shining with the slickness of ancient brine, it lashed back and forth, each movement a cataclysmic ripple that sent new waves crashing towards him. The body, vast and unknowable, remained submerged, an unseen horror connecting the human head and the serpentine tail into a single, terrifying entity that spanned the very world.

The boat tilted precariously, groaning, threatening to capsize and drag him into the frigid depths. The water screamed, a chorus of the damned, of souls lost to the hungry ocean. And Dan, who had grown used to commanding his dreams, who had woven reality from dust, could not calm the storm.

The waves towered, blotting out the last vestiges of light, crushing the very air from his lungs. Dan clung to the splintered wood, his heart a trapped bird, beating a desperate rhythm against his ribs. There was nowhere to run. No sky, no land, only the endless, hungry sea and the monstrous god that ruled it. Then, the voice, ancient and vast, echoed not from the monstrous mouth, but from the depths of Dan's own skull, a resonant thrum that vibrated through his very being.

“I am Lord Dagon,” the figure boomed, his voice a coiling, inescapable tide, dragging Dan into its depths. “I am the God thy Father hath chosen. Lord of Abundance. For this purpose was I wrought, and for this purpose have I come. I bring unto thee a final chance to deliver thyself from peril.”

Dan's fists clenched, the splinters biting into his skin, each sharp point a tiny pain that anchored him to defiance. “But I did not call you!” he choked out, his voice raw against the roar of the ocean.

“Thou hast ever called, and ever dost call. Thy people did so. Thy very flesh doth so,” Dagon rumbled, his voice laced with ancient patience, a chilling certainty. “Yet this night hast thou sinned, daring to undo My work in thy father’s house, interfering with a sacred bond betwixt man and god.” A cruel smile, vast and chilling, twisted the stone lips. “Come, then, cast thyself down, seek My forgiveness, embrace My bounty, and I shall spare thee the inevitable. I shall offer unto thee true might and protection, dominion over the very elements thou so foolishly sought to command.”

“Abundance of things?” Dan spat, his voice trembling but defiant, a small, brave spark in the face of annihilation. “Why devote my life to things I cannot carry into death? My people say your temple fell by the hands of Samson. Your statue crumbled before the Ark. Why should I choose you over El, who dwells in the heavens? You are nothing! A false promise, a lie carved from stone!”

Dagon leaned closer, his shadow swallowing the remnants of the dream, blotting out the last slivers of light. “My temple fell not for My weakness, boy. My statue crumbled not for want of My power. There exist boundaries, ancient lines which must not be transgressed. The realm of the living cannot trespass too far into the realm of the dead. We are bound by different laws. My presence nigh to that which is called holy doth crack the very illusion of thy fragile world. Mistake not My ancient restraint for weakness. Mistake not the consequence of pacts made in ages past for impotence.”
Dan's voice shook, but his gaze did not waver. “So you’ll even it. You are a deity of death! A gatekeeper to the pit.”

“Look about thee, boy,” Dagon whispered, his voice a seductive caress, as soft and treacherous as the waves. “Look at the war within thy soul. Look at the strife beyond thy meager walls. Death is not a distant shore, boy. It is the very sea wherein thou swimmest, the very air thou dost breathe. I offer unto thee a raft, a haven amidst the tempest. Choose Me, and I and I shall shield thee from the drowning. I shall bestow upon thee power over the elements themselves…”
“I’d rather drown standing than float in your shadow,” Dan interrupted with a snarl, a fierce, primal refusal burning in his chest.

A flicker of something like disappointment, a fleeting shadow of ancient melancholy, crossed Dagon's vast face, quickly replaced by a predatory hunger that intensified the roar of the sea. “Perchance thou mightest. And perchance all those whom thou holdest dear might drown with thee. Defy Me, and I shall utterly crush them. Their screams shall echo through the deepest abysses, and their very memories shall be swallowed by the ceaseless tide.”
“Then you will know that I stood,” Dan snarled, pushing against the crushing weight of the nightmare, willing his spirit to break free.

Dagon's form surged forward, a tidal wave of darkness, a monstrous jaw fit to consume him. The dream began to fracture, lines of blinding light cracking the suffocating blackness, the illusion shattering around him.

“If ever thou shouldst alter thy mind,” Dagon hissed as the dream shattered, his voice a promise and a threat that echoed into the waking world, “then merely knock thrice, ask twice, and wait. I shall come.”

Dan awoke with a strangled cry, his body slick with sweat, every muscle screaming in protest. The cot was still, surprisingly solid beneath him. His room was his again, familiar walls, familiar darkness. But the sea still roared in his ears, a phantom echo of the nightmare's depths. He sat up, gasping, the air cold and heavy, a stark contrast to the suffocating warmth of the dream. Dawn was almost upon him, a faint gray light beginning to creep through the narrow slit in the wall.
He looked toward that slit, where a sliver of the lightening sky was visible, and whispered, “Stardust to Stardust…”

Then Dan lay back down, his eyes open, staring at the unseen ceiling. A new journey awaited him, one that would take him far from his kindred and farther still from his people and their lands. But it was not distance that mattered now. It was Truth. And Dan knew he would find it, for though the sea inside him was not calm, he had learned its tides. And he had learned how to flood.
{End Chapters 1}
Chapter 2:
Living Sacrifice
“Dan!”

The scream tore through the pre-dawn stillness of Hebron, ragged and desperate. It was the sound of a man wrestling not with sleep, but with the things sleep had shown him, a man whose carefully constructed world had just been rattled to its rotten foundations.

Dan’s eyes snapped open. Sleep, thin and fleeting, vanished instantly, leaving him suspended in that disorienting space between dreaming and waking. He knew that sound. It was Aviel. His father. Stories like this, one might tell you, often begin with a cry in the night, a moment when the mundane unravels to reveal something far older, far stranger. And sometimes, those voices echo from deeper places than mere memory can fathom.

He scrambled off his cot, bare soles meeting the cool, familiar stone of his meticulously tidy room – a small island of order in the rising tide of chaos his father had allowed. He didn't hesitate. He knew the path, even through the dim light, though it wound through his father's self-made labyrinth. He needed to understand what the vision had achieved, what strange fruit his carefully planted seed of intent had borne. Or perhaps he just needed to stop the shouting, that immediate, primal need to silence the noise, for noise, you see, often draws far more attention than one desires.

He moved quickly but cautiously, bare feet stepping over unseen mounds of decaying fabric, dodging brittle stacks of pottery shards that scraped like dry bones. A muffled clink here, a rustle there. Each sound reminded him of the filth he was trying to escape, the physical manifestation of his father's spiritual decay. His heart hammered, a frantic echo of the drumbeat from before, a frantic bird trapped in a cage of stone and refuse.
He reached the doorway of his father’s room, the air thick with the stale scent of sickness and dust. His father sat bolt upright on his cot, eyes wide and wild in the gloom, chest heaving.
Dan stepped into the room, his voice quiet but firm. “Yes, Father. Here I am!”
Aviel blinked, his gaze focusing slowly. Relief warred with terror in his eyes. “Dan! You are here. Thank the... thank the lord.” He swallowed hard, his throat working. “I… I had a vision, son.”

Dan moved closer, sitting carefully on the edge of the cot beside his father. The simple straw mattress offered little comfort, but it was a place to anchor himself in the face of his father’s turmoil. “I am yours to listen, Father,” Dan said, keeping his tone neutral, though a knot of anticipation had tightened in his stomach. What had the message wrought? Had the truth, however unwelcome, finally pierced the veil of delusion? Such things, as the old ones say, are often hoped for, but rarely seen.

Aviel recounted the vision, though it was a distorted, fragmented version of the one Dan had crafted. He spoke of the gray, empty field, of dust swirling, then of pebbles and rocks falling from the sky, accumulating around him, weighing him down until he couldn’t breathe. He described the wind that swept it away, the brief glimpse of a single, distant star. He recounted the feeling more than the images – the oppressive weight, the struggle, the sudden lightness, the glimpse of something far off. He had felt the pressure, the cleansing wind, the hint of ascent… yet his mind, trapped in its familiar patterns, had twisted the map to a mountain peak into a comfortable ditch. Oh, the peculiar ways in which a man’s heart can deceive him, even when the truth is laid bare!

Aviel finished, a strange, unsettling excitement dawning in his eyes – an excitement Dan hadn't seen directed at anything real or productive in years. It was the feverish energy of a man who believes he's found a treasure map, even if the map leads nowhere good. "It is a sign, Dan! A clear sign! The weight... the accumulation... it was showing me this place! This house! It is time, son of my flesh, it is time for me to cast off this burden! The vision tells me I must leave! I must go and find my path, away from here!"

He began to scramble off the cot, fumbling for the worn sack he kept by his bed, the one containing his few precious, worthless possessions. It was as if he planned to walk out into the dark morning right now, leaving everything behind as easily as shedding a cloak.

Dan stared, aghast. His father, leaving? Abandoning everything, including him, based on that interpretation? It made no sense! Aviel was the market man, the scholar, the one with responsibilities, however neglected they might be now. He was tied to the very fabric of Hebron, however frayed. His departure, Dan knew, would unravel more than just their household. It would affect the very threads of the city itself, though the townsfolk would only mutter of it later, or perhaps never. One can never be certain of what whispers on the wind when the great pieces begin to move.

"Father!" Dan reached out, having the nerve to grab Aviel by the shoulders, shaking him gently but firmly. "Father, have you gone senile?! Have you finally lost it all?! This is my journey! The vision was for me! It showed my path, clearing away the things holding me down! Look at me! I am able-bodied! Send me!"

Aviel froze, his hands still fumbling with the sack. The excitement drained from his face, replaced first by a familiar sadness, deep and bone-weary. To lose his son now, after losing Zuna, after the emptiness that consumed him… it was too much. But then, as Dan’s words sank in – the mention of the vision being his, the implicit rejection of Aviel’s current path, the audacity of the boy claiming a journey the father had misinterpreted for himself – the sadness curdled. It turned into something sharper, colder. Recognition flickered in Aviel's eyes, then hardened into understanding and, finally, anger. He saw the truth: Dan wasn't just leaving the house; he was leaving the world his father had chosen, a house of Dagon.

"Your journey?" Aviel's voice dropped, losing its frantic edge and gaining a dangerous steel. "Is this about... chasing after some god? You dare send me visions in my sleep, disrupting the peace, simply because you scorn Lord Dagon?!" He spat the words, laced with the old bitterness. "If you
are
still
looking for a god
," he quoted himself, the words heavy with accusation, "then pick up a rock and make it so!"

The air in the small room grew heavy, not with dust, but with the weight of years unsaid. Accusations, sharp as flint, flew from Aviel's lips, each one landing like a physical blow. "Still wrestling with your gods, are we?" Aviel sneered, his voice edged with bitter amusement. "Or is it just the one, now? The mighty Dagon, perhaps? Did you think I wouldn't know?" His father's face, already drawn by fear, hardened, a muscle jumping in his jaw, and his eyes, once wild, now held a chilling, wounded glint as they raked over Dan, as if the boy's very posture was a silent, damning indictment. Dan watched, a cold knot tightening in his gut, as Aviel's trembling fingers tightened on the tattered sack by his bed, a desperate grip on the very things that chained him. The truth, starkly revealed in a dream, seemed to shatter against a mind fiercely barricaded, leaving no impression. The room pulsed with the unspoken, two opposing currents in the same river: one sinking deeper into the suffocating comfort of rot, the other straining, every sinew taut, to break free and climb towards the vast, terrifying silence of the unknown.

Finally, Aviel’s face set in grim resolve. The grief was buried again under layers of anger and stubborn pride. "Then so be it!" he declared, his voice shaking with suppressed fury. "If this house, if I, are such a burden to you, if you must chase after gods in the sky like a fool... then yes! You must go! Pack your things! Leave this house! Leave Hebron! Leave immediately!" He leaned forward, his voice dropping to a harsh whisper, laced with a vindictive satisfaction that chilled Dan to the bone. "And may whatever god you seek protect you from the consequences of spurning Lord Dagon this night! For even a god of the depths has a reach you cannot fathom!"

Stunned but strangely resolute, Dan stood. There was nothing more to say, nothing more to do here. His desperate mission to shake his father awake had failed, spectacularly. Instead, it had propelled him toward the inevitable, like a stone finally dislodged from a cliff face.

He turned and walked back down the dreadful hallway, the silence that followed Aviel's outburst even heavier than the previous gloom. He made his way to his small room, the haven of order, and began to gather his few necessary possessions. From a neatly folded pile, he took a sturdy tunic and breeches, rolling them tight. He then secured his thick wool cloak against the night chill, and a simple, tightly woven blanket that would serve as his sleeping mat if called for. He tied these into a compact roll, preparing his basic necessities for the road.

Next, he moved to his small, worn leather satchel. Into it went a small pouch of dried figs or cured olives, a precious pinch of salt, and the flint and pyrite for fire. (The exact contents of that pouch? A detail lost to the mists of time, for now. But rest assured, it will matter.) His mother's small, smooth carving stone – a tactile link to the stillness she had taught him – nestled safely within. He took his small leather pouch, adding the twenty shekels he had earned working in the vineyards with his father and convincing Aviel to part with things they could actually sell rather than hoard – hard-earned and harder saved.

As he prepared to leave the house, standing at the threshold, his father appeared in the hallway, looking slightly less wild, the anger now mixed with a deep, visible sadness. The brief storm had passed, leaving behind the familiar landscape of his grief.

"Where do you go, son?" Aviel asked, his voice rough, eyes still searching, as if for the last echoes of his dream. "The vision... it spoke of stars, did it not? Of burdens shed, of rising." He gestured vaguely north, his hand sweeping across the unseen landscape beyond their walls. "Always up. From these southern hills, through the lowlands, past the salty waters, and then ascend to the fresh waters of the Sea of Galilee. From there, to Dan, at the base of Mount Hermon, and then to its very tops, where the veil is thinnest." His voice grew quiet, almost reflective, as if reciting an ancient map of the soul, a path laid out by generations of travelers and whispered legends. "From the high places, through the valleys, to the highest peaks. Where the boundary between the world of men and the places unseen is most fragile.”

Dan's breath caught. He stared at his father, a strange, almost knowing look on his face. The words resonated, a sudden, undeniable clarity. Dan. His namesake city. The path, once vague, now etched itself in his mind with the certainty of prophecy. He held his father's gaze. "Yes, Father," he said, his voice firm, a new resolve settling in his chest. "Up. To Dan! My namesake city!"

Aviel reached into his own simple pouch and pulled out five additional shekels, pressing them into Dan's hand. "For reserve," he said gruffly. "For bribe, should you need it. The world outside... it is not kind to fools chasing heights." He also pressed a heavy, aged bottle of wine into his other hand. "From a colleague at the market. A fine aged wine from the hills. Good stuff. Don't squander it." A dreadful vintage, truth be told. It tasted like fermented dust and regret. The mysteries of human tastes remain quite profound. And finally, a familiar leather water pouch – the kind used in these lands for centuries, a simple, necessary vessel. "Take this. You will need water."

Dan accepted the gifts, the weight of them heavy in his hands, heavier still with the complex emotions swirling between them. This was a farewell, perhaps a final one. A father, lost in his own descent, now, through these very gifts, propelled his son towards an ascent Aviel could only watch from below, a painful severing of ties for a greater, unknown purpose. It was a paradox, a final, twisted act of love and resignation.
"Go now," Aviel said, stepping back. "Before I change my mind."

He checked the small, heavy dagger tucked into his simple belt, the one his father had taught him to use when he was younger. A good memory, that. Aviel, before the dust claimed him, patient and steady, showing a much smaller Dan how to hold the blade, how to carve simple shapes from wood, how to hone an edge. It made Dan's chest ache now, remembering that time, that different man.

He secured his leather water container and the heavy, aged bottle of wine to his side, then picked up the sturdy walking staff leaning against the wall, and finally, a length of strong, thin rope coiled neatly at its base. He pushed the ache down and continued onward.

Dan turned and stepped out into the cool early morning air of Hebron. The city was just beginning to stir. Hebron itself, a city of old stone and older dust, smelled of spices, dry earth and something ancient. The taste of the air was thin and dry. The sounds of the waking city were the low murmur of voices, the clanking of buckets at wells, the rhythmic thud of a weaver's loom, the sharp strike of a blacksmith's hammer from a nearby alley, the bustle of vendors setting up their stalls, the soft padding of sandals on stone, the distant braying of a donkey. As he made his way through the familiar streets towards the northern gate, the stone felt cool and worn through his sandals. The light was still soft, painting the stone walls in hues of rose and gold. He passed by the marketplace, quiet now, but in the hours to come, a cacophony of shouting merchants and braying animals. He saw a man by the side of the road, already at work, standing beside a small group of donkeys. “Asses! Only half a shekel or fair trade today only!" the man screamed, his voice raw in the morning air. Dan noted the price – astonishingly low – but kept walking. His journey was on foot, a sacrifice, a shedding of ease.

Further on, as he neared the edge of the city, he saw a small procession. A noble, perhaps, or a wealthy merchant, walking with an air of self-importance, surrounded by a small retinue of servants, and yes, musicians playing flutes and drums, and dancers, their movements fluid even in the soft morning light. They moved with a kind of cushioned ease, a bubble of comfort in the harsh world. Dan watched them pass. Could a person like that go up? He wondered. Could they shed the weight of their possessions, their status, their easy lives? He looked at the noble's face, impassive in the growing light, and understood, with a pang of sorrow, that he could not read the heart behind the eyes. He did not know their burdens, or their dreams.

As he made his way towards the northern gate, the city walls loomed ahead, a fortress of ancient stone against the wild, awakening hills. He stopped briefly at the homes of his closest friends, the ones whose laughter still echoed in his memory, whispering quick, tearful goodbyes through closed doors. He promised to return someday, knowing even as he spoke the words that the boy who left tonight would not be the same one who might come back. He was leaving behind more than just a house; he was leaving a life, a childhood, and the very air he had breathed since birth.

He stepped through the final arch of the northern gate, the cool, worn stone brushing his shoulder like a final, silent goodbye. The massive timbers of the gate groaned softly as he passed, a guttural sigh of farewell. The city's sounds, once a comforting hum, now seemed to recede instantly, swallowed by the vast, open expanse of the Judean wilderness. He paused, just outside the threshold, turning for one last glance at the familiar silhouette of Hebron's walls, stark against the lightening sky. A lone goat bleated from a distant hillside, its cry echoing in the new stillness, a stark contrast to the human bustle he had just left. The air here was cleaner, sharper, carrying the wild scent of dry grass and ancient rock, tasting of freedom and the unknown.

His feet, accustomed to the city's paved paths, now met the rougher, uneven earth of the ancient road. The path stretched before him, disappearing into the pre-dawn gloom, beckoning him onward. He took a deep breath, filling his lungs with the crisp, cool air, and then, without looking back, began to walk.

The ancient road north from Hebron, a ribbon of worn stone and packed earth, wound through vineyards and olive groves, their leaves shimmering silver in the nascent light. Beyond these cultivated lands, it opened onto rockier, more unforgiving terrain, the path itself a testament to countless footsteps over millennia. He walked through the long hours of the day, the sun climbing higher, transforming from a gentle eye to a blazing, furious orb, a molten disc of pure rage, beating down on the Earth with its relentless, unforgiving heat. The landscape blurred into a shimmering haze of heat and dust, the distant hills rippling like water.

He passed workers heading to fields and shepherds leading flocks into Tekoa, a city just off his path. As the sun began its slow descent, painting the sky in hues of orange and purple, Dan passed near a valley of springs and caverns, a place whispered about by locals, who spoke of a prophecy that one day "a king of peace" would carve great pools there to bring life to the desert.

Sweat beaded on his brow and trickled down his back, a constant, irritating stream. His throat grew dry, a persistent dryness that promised worse to come. The water pouch, once reassuringly full, now felt lighter with every step, its contents dwindling with alarming speed. The dust, fine and insidious, coated his tongue. His muscles began to ache with the steady, unyielding rhythm of the walk, a dull throb that spread from his calves to his hips.
With each rhythmic thud of his sturdy walking staff against the earth, Dan felt the rhythm of the journey settle into his bones.
Up
, his father had said,
to Dan, the base of Mount Hermon, and then to its very tops, where the veil is thinnest.

The words echoed, a strange, compelling prophecy. He thought of the squalor he'd left, the suffocating weight of his father's house, the dust that seemed to cling to everything and everyone. This road, though harsh, felt clean. Every ache, every bead of sweat, felt like a cleansing, a shedding of the old skin. He was not just walking away from Hebron; he was walking
into
something, stepping into the vast, unknown expanse his father had only hinted at.

Such journeys, dear reader, are not merely measured by the miles beneath one's feet. Oh no, not at all. They are, rather, about the turning of the soul, the shedding of the past, and the brave, foolish hope for what lies beyond the next horizon. This is a truth often observed by those who watch such tales unfold. He kept a steady pace, the wine bottle and water pouch bouncing softly against his side, a counterpoint to the thrumming anticipation in his chest. He was on his way. On his way up.

Bethlehem was near, and what appeared to be a well was right ahead next to a cluster of olive trees and moving figures. His thirst was a burning ache now, a constant reminder of his empty vessel. As he drew closer, one of the figures, this one a woman, detached herself from the others. She wore bright clothes and an even brighter smile. Her clothing was scanty, revealing more of herself than was considered decent among his people, or indeed, most people of that time. Well-kept hair flowed down to her waist and danced around her like a mist. She approached him just before he reached that savior of a well, the stone worn smooth by countless hands and ropes over centuries.

"Well, now," she purred, her voice like honey poured over warm stones, her gaze lingering on his dusty face. "A young man, clearly on a grand mission. And so terribly thirsty, aren't you, my dear? Don't you worry your head about it. Let me just fetch you a drink." She smiled, a slow, knowing curl of her lips, and her eyes, bright as fresh coins, were utterly alluring, pulling him in. He noticed she was not one of his people; her features, her accent, marked her as a foreigner. She told him she had traveled west from Edom, a place where, as he recalled, warnings had been whispered about "muddying" with certain women.

But when thirst envelops the mind, one loses direction. They went to the well together. She lowered the bucket, the rope creaking, and drew up cool water. She offered him a drink, her hand brushing his as he took the cup, a touch that lingered, light as a butterfly wing. He drank deeply, splashing some cool water on his face, the blessed cold startling him.

"Ah, that is much better, is it not, sweet pilgrim?" she purred, drawing near, and the very air around him thickened, growing heavy with her scent—jasmine and something else, something primal and rich, like warm earth after rain. Her fingers, light as a moth's wing, brushed his shoulder, and a jolt, quite electric, seemed to pass from her skin to his. "The road is long and hard for one so young, precious. There are so many easier paths. Paths with comfort, with joy, with... delightful companionship." She leaned closer, her voice a conspiratorial murmur, her eyes, bright as freshly minted coins, holding his gaze, promising unspoken wonders. She spun a silken web of words: of rest, of soft beds, of forgetting the dust of the road in surroundings far, far softer, of pleasures that would make the journey seem like a distant, unpleasant dream.

And oh, how the very air around her shimmered, promising all manner of sweet forgetting. Dan felt a dizzying warmth bloom in his chest, a pull originating deep in his bones, a current dragging at his thoughts, clouding them as surely as a desert wind clouds the sky. His vision blurred at the edges, his very senses conspiring against him, drawn downwards by an unseen, insistent force, leaving his resolve a wavering candle flame in a sudden gust. It was a terrible, beautiful thing, the way her presence seemed to resonate with a fundamental part of him, a forgotten melody, a deep, ancient call. To deny it felt like tearing a piece from his very soul, a most unnatural thing, you understand, for who would turn from such a feast? Yet, even as the heat burned, a chill certainty began to spread through him. This was no mere woman, no simple roadside encounter. Oh no. This was the very essence of the things he fled, a shimmering, living embodiment of easy pleasure, of entanglement, of distraction from the upward path. He knew, with a certainty that settled cold and sharp in his gut, that this was a crucible, a test, perhaps, sent by powers who disliked his chosen direction.

He stopped, met her gaze with calm respect, and spoke words that surprised even himself with their firmness, words that felt borrowed from a place of deeper strength. "Peace to you, woman from Edom," he said. "But my path lies elsewhere, and I cannot stop. May you find what you seek." He gave her a small, polite bow, and turned back to the well. The woman from Edom watched him, her smile fading, replaced by a look of cold, sharp surprise. Without another word, she turned and walked back to the group she had come from, melting back into their midst.

Dan felt the residual heat of her presence slowly recede, leaving behind a faint chill. He was still thirsty, bone-dry and weary, but the air around him now seemed sharper, purer. He had said no. He had pushed back against a subtle, insidious pull, and in that refusal, he felt a spark of something new and fierce ignite within him. A small victory, yes, but a victory nonetheless, on a road that promised many more battles unseen. He needed more water, desperately, but more than that, he needed clarity. He needed to wash away the last lingering traces of her intoxicating presence. He began to lower the bucket. As he looked down into the dark mouth of the well, seeking his own dusty, weary reflection, something happened.
A ripple.
It spread across the surface, though nothing had disturbed the water. The ripple grew into small waves, swirling faster and faster. And then, instead of his face, the water showed… something else…
{End Chapters 2}
Chapter 3:
Lift Up

Something was wrong. The well water, just moments before a promise of cold, clean clarity, had twisted into a mirror for something else entirely. It was a phenomenon so unsettling, so utterly other, that Dan's weary mind, already pushed to its limits by the day's trials, struggled to process it. The image that had coalesced was not merely a reflection, but a shimmering, impossible vision, rising from the dark, still depths.

A being of impossible beauty, a form perfectly sculpted from the very dreams one dared not whisper, and indeed, dear reader, they would appear to you precisely as your heart's most secret longing. They were adorned not merely with jewels, but with the very essence of them: rubies that pulsed with the warmth of a lover's heart, emeralds that gleamed with Spring’s promise of green pastures, and sapphires that held the cool, deep mystery of the night sky. Symbols of fertility and abundance, carved from ancient bone and polished stone, seemed to float around them, each one humming with a silent, vibrant energy. Their eyes, luminous and ancient, burned with an intensity that dwarfed any simple allure; they were pools of starlight and shadowed desire, drawing one in, promising the culmination of every secret longing. This was no mere flesh and blood. This was a goddess woven from the very fabric of life's deepest hungers.

“I am Lady Astarte,” the figure boomed, their voice a coiling, inescapable tide, dragging Dan into its fertile, intoxicating depths. “I am the Lord of Fertility. For this purpose was I wrought, and for this purpose have I come. I bring unto thee the truth of thy barren striving, and the true path to deliverance.”

Their presence pulsed from the water, a silent, powerful energy, a force that resonated with the very lifeblood in his veins, a hum that seemed to vibrate in the hollows of his bones. And they spoke, their voice a sound woven from moonlight and ripe fields, from the laughter of lovers and the cries of newborns, from the rustle of silk and the murmur of ancient rivers. It was a sound that bypassed his ears entirely and echoed directly in the deepest chambers of his soul, a melody both terrifying and irresistibly sweet.

They smiled, a slow, devastating expression that promised every earthly delight, every comfort, every forgotten joy. "Thou didst reject my follower on the road," they purred, their voice laced with cold amusement and simmering fury, a subtle, dangerous undertow beneath the honeyed words. "That one was a vessel of My power, a taste of the abundance I offer. And thou didst turn away. Thou didst scorn the flesh, the very fount of life and joy. For this cause am I here, child. To reveal unto thee the folly of thy chosen path."

The well water swirled faster, the reflection of Astarte growing clearer, more vibrant, almost painfully so, as if the very air was being drawn into her image. The scent of sweet jasmine and rich earth rose from the well, mingling with the dry desert air, a heady, disorienting perfume that promised oblivion. Dan stumbled back, his hands scraping on the rough stone edge of the well. His mind reeled. This was not a dream. This was real. And it was far more powerful than any vision his father had ever described. His body, already weary from the long day's march, from the relentless sun and the gnawing thirst, felt a sudden, profound weakness, a vulnerability that craved nothing more than relief. He had walked alone, and the solitude, though chosen, had begun to gnaw at the edges of his resolve.

"Thy path is one of emptiness," Astarte continued, their voice a silken ribbon unspooling in his mind, drawing him in even as he tried to resist. "Of denial. Thou seekest to shed the bonds of the world, to ascend to some barren, distant height. But I tell thee, sweetness is found in the depths, in the full embrace of life. The soil, the vine, the blossoming womb – these are my domain. And they are joy." The words were not merely heard; they were felt, a warm current flowing through his veins, whispering of ease. "Why seek the cold, distant stars when the warmth of human touch awaits? Why strive for lonely heights when the softest bed, the fullest meal, the laughter of companionship, can be thine for the taking? This journey of yours, this solitary quest, it is a foolish thing, a denial of the very essence of what it means to live. Life, dear boy, is found in connection, in the weaving of souls, in the shared warmth against the night's chill. It is in the gentle hand that offers comfort, the soft breath against your cheek, the deep, resonant hum of belonging. These are the gifts I offer, the true abundance."

The image in the water pulsed, and Dan felt a sudden, intense yearning bloom in his chest, a hunger that went beyond mere physical thirst. It was a desire for comfort, for warmth, for the softness of skin and the sweetness of ripe fruit, for the simple, undeniable solace of not being alone. The parched dryness of his journey, the ache in his muscles, the gnawing hunger – all of it seemed to cry out for the very things Astarte offered. It was a tempting vision, seductive beyond words, a return to all the senses he had just tried to deny. For a fleeting, desperate moment, he wanted nothing more than to dive into that well, to drown in the intoxicating warmth of Astarte’s promised abundance. Indeed, Dan almost fell into the well that day. For even the stoutest heart, when faced with such overwhelming sensual delight after a long and arid road, might falter. Consider this, for temptation, you see, is often the siren call of ease, a cunning absence of higher good, dressed in velvet and whispering sweet nothings.

But then, a grounding image flashed in his mind: his father, trapped in the dusty squalor of his own choices, suffocated by the very weight of "things." The stagnant air, the rotting piles, the festering spiritual decay. Decay was all that earthly treasures wrought. He remembered the clarity of the starry night from his own vision, the feeling of soaring, of shedding burdens, of finding the unburdened way. He remembered the chill, pure air of the peaks, the vast, boundless sky.

He lifted his head, forcing his gaze away from the swirling depths of the well, fixing his eyes on the distant horizon, where the faint glimmer of the first stars was beginning to appear against the fading twilight.

"I'm not seeking emptiness, Lady Astarte," Dan said, his voice raw but firm, cutting through her potent allure. He looked them straight in the eye. "My path isn't about missing out. It's about getting free, getting clear. Your abundance, for all its glow, feels heavy. It clings. It pulls you down, ties you to the earth. It's a comfort of the flesh that just clogs the spirit, a pleasure that buries what truly matters beneath a haze of sensation. I need to be light. I want truth that's found by stripping away, not by grasping more. Not by rooting oneself in the dirt, but by rising above it. Your gifts, for all their beauty, are just a gilded cage built of bodily desires."

Astarte's beautiful face in the water contorted, ever so slightly, a flash of pure, cold fury passing through their luminous eyes. The seductive purr in their voice hardened, gaining an edge of ancient, terrible power.

"A trap, child? Nay. It is the very tapestry of existence. Thou art but a thread, and thou dost presume to sever thyself from the weave? Foolish boy! Thy pride shall be thy undoing. To ascend to the barren, to float among the cold, distant lights… is to know truly nothing. It is to know the bleakest of all solitudes."

The well water began to boil, sending up plumes of fragrant steam that coated Dan's face like a shroud, burning his skin. The beautiful, terrible image of Astarte expanded, filling his vision, their face contorting into something monstrously beautiful, a terrifying amalgam of seductive goddess and ancient, furious deity. Jewels gleamed like predatory eyes, and symbols of fertility twisted into grotesque, writhing patterns, pulsing with malevolent energy. The air grew thick and heavy, almost viscous, making it hard to breathe, each inhale a struggle against an unseen weight. He felt an invisible pressure pressing down on him, a crushing force that threatened to drive him back down into the earth, to flatten him into the very dust he sought to escape. It was the same feeling he’d crafted for his father’s dream, but amplified a thousandfold, aimed directly at him, seeking to break his spirit and his body. The very ground beneath his feet seemed to tremble, resonating with her wrath.

"Thou art a frail, trembling thing!" Astarte’s voice boomed, no longer a purr but a resonant gong striking against the inside of his skull, vibrating through his teeth and bones. "To turn from the warmth of the soil, the embrace of the living, for what? For empty air? For the cold, indifferent gaze of distant stars? I tell thee, boy, the cosmos cares not for thy puny aspirations! Thou art born of the earth, and to the earth shalt thou return!"

The pressure intensified, forcing him to his knees beside the well. His head swam, and black spots danced before his eyes, threatening to swallow the last vestiges of light. He felt a profound sense of loss, as if something essential were being ripped from him, piece by agonizing piece, a vital cord severed. This was the moment, he realized dimly, the true test. This was the true cost of refusing such overwhelming power, such ancient comfort, such a fundamental promise of belonging. The path upward was a lonely one, and it demanded everything, stripping him bare.

But even as the darkness threatened to consume him, a grounding image pierced through the terror. Not from the well, but from deep within his own mind, a memory, or perhaps something more. The image of the single, distant star from his vision, the one his father had dismissed, now grew brighter, pulsating with a gentle, inviting light. He heard Zuna’s voice, faint but clear, speaking words he hadn’t thought about in years: “Up, my little pigeon. Always up.” He remembered the suffocating weight of his father's house, the clinging burden of things, and the stark, painful truth that true freedom lay in shedding, not accumulating. He gritted his teeth, digging his fingers into the rough stone of the well-curb. His muscles screamed with the effort, but he refused to fall entirely. His head was bowed, but his spirit was not broken. He remembered his purpose. .

"No," Dan whispered, his voice hoarse, barely audible above the booming wrath of Astarte, but resolute. "I will not return. I seek the light. I seek the truth. I will not be pulled down by the burdens of the flesh, nor the comforting lies of the earth!”

As Dan uttered those words, a furious, chilling shriek tore through the air from the well, a sound that cracked the very stones and sent a shiver of primordial dread down his spine. It was the sound of ancient power denied, of cosmic rage unleashed. The image of Astarte in the water flared, their beautiful, terrifying face dissolving into pure, blinding light, then twisting into a vortex of swirling dark water. "If ever thou shouldst need me, child, merely knock thrice, ask twice, and I shall—" The scent of jasmine and earth curdled into something acrid, like burnt offerings and decaying flowers. The well water roiled violently, surging over the edge, drenching Dan in a wave of cold, muddy spray that smelled not of life, but of death and corruption. He gasped, choking, spitting out the foul water.

When he looked up, gasping for breath, the well was still. The water was once more placid, reflecting only the distant, innocent stars beginning to pierce the twilight sky. The air, though still holding a faint, metallic tang, had lost its oppressive weight. Astarte was gone, her challenge met, her ancient power rebuffed.

Dan stood, shaky but resolute, his clothes plastered to his skin, shivering despite the lingering heat of the day. He had faced a god, or something very much like one, and had not broken. The exhaustion was bone-deep, but beneath it, a strange, exhilarating lightness began to hum within him. The very ground felt different under his feet, less like solid earth and more like a launching pad. Indeed, when one refuses the easy path, the world itself seems to shift, offering a firmer footing for the journey ahead.
He closed his eyes for a moment, then opened them and looked down into the well again. Only his own dusty, weary face looked back from the water. He had refused a goddess.
"Harlot!" he spat, the word raw and trembling, directed at the lingering presence, the fading image, the sheer audacity of the temptation.

Twice in one day, the world had presented him with paths of ease, cloaked in power and comfort, and twice he had turned his back. The confrontation with Astarte had drained him more than the walk from Hebron, leaving him trembling, but a strange, unyielding core had hardened within him. A new strength, sharp and unyielding, seemed to hum just beneath his skin, a force forged in the crucible of divine temptation. He felt it, a nascent 'will,' a simple name for a complex, powerful force.

He reached Bethlehem as the light faded and night began to fall, the hills around the town softening into shadow like sleeping giants. He found a shepherd's shelter willing to offer him a corner for the night and shared a meager meal of bread and cheese with the family. The low murmur of voices from nearby homes and the distant bleating of sheep on the hillsides, sounds as comforting as an old lullaby, drew him outside the shelter for a moment.

Bethlehem, even as evening settled, felt older, tied to the quiet rhythm of flocks and fields, but also expectant, alive with whispers of the times. It felt like a place rooted in the soil, dreaming ancient dreams. He saw a few townsfolk gathered near a lamp hanging by a doorway, their faces illuminated in the soft glow, looking like faces carved from warm wood as they spoke in hushed tones. Curiosity, that familiar companion, drew him closer. He lingered just within earshot, listening.

Dan listened, the talk of leaders and uncertain futures a distant echo against the dream-battles he had just fought. He heard a man declare, "King David! A true warrior, that one! He'll bring us peace, mark my words!" A woman's voice, softer but firm, added, "No more Philistines raiding our threshing floors, not with him on the throne! The High Power is clearly with him, guiding his hand!" Another chimed in, "Born right here, in Bethlehem soil, he was! He won't forget us!" The quiet talk wove through admiration for his deeds and a soft anticipation of what his continued leadership might mean. "A brighter future, that's what he promises," someone murmured, "a true king for all Israel!" It is a strange thing, is it not, how the hopes of ordinary folk hang on the actions of those destined for greatness? Yet, Dan understood the yearning in their voices – not for a change in who ruled them, but for the promise of security and prosperity that a strong, chosen leader represented. It was a different kind of yearning than his own ascent, but perhaps rooted in the same soil – a desire for a higher purpose to be manifest in the world, even in earthly leadership. He stood there for a while longer, absorbing the quiet hope and underlying anticipation of the town, feeling the weight of their collective outlook, like carrying a basket full of wishes. He then returned inside the shelter, lay down on a rough cot, the conversations of the townsfolk fading into the background like distant music, soft and indistinct.

Dan listened, the talk of leaders and uncertain futures a distant echo against the battles he had just fought. Yet, he understood the yearning in their voices – not for a change in who ruled them, but for the promise of security and prosperity that a strong, chosen leader represented. It was a different kind of yearning than his own ascent, but perhaps rooted in the same soil – a desire for a higher purpose to be manifest in the world, even in earthly leadership. He stood there for a while longer, absorbing the quiet hope and underlying anticipation of the town, feeling the weight of their collective outlook, like carrying a basket full of wishes. He then returned inside the shelter, lay down on a rough cot, the conversations of the townsfolk fading into the background like distant music, soft and indistinct.

The exhaustion was immense, a heavy weight, but the strange lightness, the exhilaration born of resistance and resolve, still shimmered beneath it. He had held his ground against the very forces that sought to pull him down or root him firmly in the earth, and that defiance felt like wings.

Sleep claimed him instantly. It was a deep sleep, deeper than any he had known before, uncluttered by the anxieties of the house or the lingering presence of dark gods. He slipped effortlessly into the Dreamscape, but this time, he was not navigating a desolate field or a stormy sea. He was already moving. Up.

He rose with incredible speed, leaving the sleeping city of Bethlehem far below, a tiny cluster of lights swallowed by the receding earth. The ground fell away with dizzying speed, the familiar hills flattening, the curved horizon becoming ever more pronounced. He shot through the atmosphere, the air thinning to nothingness, the sounds of the world fading to an absolute, profound silence. He shot past clouds, vast and swirling like celestial oceans, painted in impossible colours by the sun he was rapidly approaching.

The moon, a colossal pearl, grew rapidly in his vision, a pockmarked, silent world hanging in the blackness. He passed it in an instant, and he knew, with undeniable certainty, that the moon smiled at him. He felt a pull, a strange, insistent tug, as if the moon were trying to draw him in, almost like a great stone in the sky pulling at the water below. But the very core of him, hardened by refusal and lightened by shedding, offered no purchase to its ancient grasp. He was able to go even higher. Then the sun itself began to shrink, becoming just another bright star as he left the inner reaches of their small system.

And then, the countless stars erupted around him. This was new, higher than he had ever reached before. They didn't appear one by one, scattered across a distant canvas. Instead, as his speed surged, the very air around him seemed to stretch and twist, pulling him forward. The stars ahead, towards which he was hurtling, didn't just get brighter; they converged, their light shifting into piercing, brilliant points that crowded together, forming a blinding, shimmering field directly in front of him. It was like plunging into a waterfall of pure light, each drop a distant sun, and a gasp tore from his chest, swallowed by the profound silence. The stars to his sides, meanwhile, seemed to stretch and smear into long, thin streaks of light, like cosmic rain falling past him with impossible speed.

He flew through their midst, sensing the chilling emptiness around him, yet completely bathed in their ancient, pure light. The silence was absolute, broken only by the silent rush of the light streaks as they blurred past. He saw nebulae not as distant clouds, but as vast, swirling cathedrals of gas and dust, painted in colours that had no names in the waking world – electric blues that hummed with unseen energy, fiery oranges that burned with silent heat, deep, velvety purples that swallowed light – structures so immense they dwarfed any mountain, any city, any world he had ever known. He was among the stars which had been waiting patiently, the ones his people had named and told stories about for generations.

A tremor of pure excitement coursed through him, a wonder so vast it made his heart ache. He felt utterly insignificant, a tiny speck, yet perfectly at home in this cosmic ocean. He was a mote of dust, yes, but dust made of this very stuff, returning to its source. He was a vessel, emptied and ready to be filled with this light. He felt no fear here, only awe and a profound sense of belonging. This was the threshold of everything he had pursued. He had aimed for the highest possible good he could conceive, the point furthest from the burdens and entanglements of his father's house, and his dreams, fueled by his newfound will, had carried him here. He had reached the stars. He hadn’t gone past them, not tonight. This was enough for now. To simply be among them, to witness their silent, rushing glory, was more than he had ever hoped. The thrill of it made his spirit soar even higher.

And I, precious reader, watching from my own quiet corner of this unfolding tale, saw him there among the cosmic dust from which he claimed humanity came. A small boy, a nascent judge, charting his upward course through the infinite. It was a sight that offered a glimmer of light in the perpetual twilight I inhabit.

He still didn't understand the full meaning of his journey, nor the ultimate destination of his ascent. He didn't know what lay ahead, or what other ancient beings might test his resolve. But he knew, with a certainty that resonated in his very soul, that he was no longer just running from something. He was moving towards something. Something immense and true and utterly beyond the confines of earthly desires. He was moving towards the stars, and the stars, he now knew, were moving towards him. The night was young, and the road, though unseen, called to him with a new, quiet urgency. The boy from Hebron, whose life had been measured in burdens and entanglements, looked up at the boundless expanse above him, and for the first time, felt truly, gloriously free. But a chill, sharp as a winter wind, touched him even in that moment of triumph. Freedom, he suspected, was just another kind of vastness, and the stars, for all their beckoning light, were cold, indifferent eyes in the endless dark. He was aiming up, all right. But what, exactly, was waiting for him at the top?
{End Chapters 3}
Chapter 4: Pilgramage

The dark, it has a way of seeping in. Not just the absence of light, but the quiet dread that settles in the corners of the mind when the world goes silent. It doesn't care if one is rich or poor, or if a smile could charm the birds from the trees. It just waits, patient as a spider in its web, for the moment one lets their guard down, for the sliver of primal fear that creeps in when the light begins to fade. And for young Dan, with eyes that saw too much and a heart that felt everything, the night had been a long, whispering journey indeed, though light danced in his vision from distant stars, bright sentinels in the cosmic dark. He stirred slowly beneath the thin, scratchy blanket, a heavy weariness clutching at him more stubbornly than any dream's fading tendrils. The straw beneath his tightly woven blanket, sharp as an ill-tempered porcupine, pressed its coarse truth against his tired bones, a constant, nagging reminder that sleep had been a wrestling match with unseen adversaries rather than a sweet surrender. It wasn't the comfort of oblivion that made rising a slow, reluctant chore, but the lingering weight of shadows, thick and cold as winter’s breath, that seemed to cling to his very soul.

The exhaustion was not merely the kind that settles in one's legs after a long day of fetching water, the sort that a good meal and a few hours' slumber could easily remedy. No, this was the deep, marrow-aching sort, a weariness born of a strange, arduous journey. For in his dream, his mind, nimble as a fox through thorny undergrowth, had soared upward, leaving his earthly vessel far below, straining towards heights that defied the very concept of "up." That arduous, invisible ascent, stretching his mind's eye towards the glinting pinpricks of distant stars, had drained him utterly. His mind felt worn thin, like a threadbare tunic, and his body, consequently, mirrored that profound fatigue with a weary sigh. He blinked against the shy, dim light that crept, cautious as a thief, through the small window, squinting as if to scrub away the haze still clinging stubbornly to his thoughts. The room smelled faintly of dust and ancient, cool stone, a scent of ages, and the city, a distant, persistent murmur, began to stir under the shy, early light, its low hum the first breath of a new day.

Dan threw his legs off the edge of the cot, a low groan escaping his lips, and stretched, muscles protesting with tiny, muffled complaints from the night’s unrest. He dressed quickly, feeling the persistent chill of the stone room seep into his skin like a silent complaint. He wrapped his rough wool cloak tighter, pulling the fabric close as he moved towards the doorway. Outside, the first blushing whisper of dawn began to settle softly over the rooftops of Bethlehem, painting the distant hills with a fleeting, golden promise. It was still quite early, the sort of time when the world feels like it’s just waking up, stretching out before the full clamor of the day truly begins. The sun, a shy, tentative golden orb, was still hugging the eastern horizon, its light spreading like spilled honey.

He held a direction in his mind, clear and unwavering as the sky overhead. His journey led him north, towards the great, rugged heights of the Jebusite lands—a place he had only glimpsed in the strange, shimmering tapestry of dreams and heard about in hushed, fireside tales. Dan had never traveled this far, only as far north as this very town of Bethlehem, and eastward to the great, still expanse of the Salt Sea, its surface like polished obsidian. The road ahead would be long, and indeed, the journey already felt vast, the hours stretching thin and quiet, measured only by the shifting light and the steady rhythm of his wandering thoughts, a low, constant murmur in his mind.

The path led him first through the highlands of Gilo, a place where the air grew thin and crisp, sharp enough to make his lungs ache with cold exhilaration, and the wind, a playful sprite, tugged at his cloak as if trying to lead him astray. Here, ancient watchtowers, like silent sentinels carved from the very rock, stood guard, their weathered stones steeped in centuries of watching, their empty eyes gazing over the vast, empty plains. Below, deep in the earth's bosom, a vast valley stretched, a place whispered about in hushed tones, where the very soil seemed to hum with old, forgotten stories. The people going to and from this valley were truly enormous, a head taller, even, than the sturdy folk of Hebron, their shadows stretching long and imposing in the sun's glare. Dan passed it by, a subtle shiver tracing its way down his spine, for some stories are best left undisturbed, their secrets kept by the wind and the ancient stones. He moved steadily onward, hour after hour, the land slowly beginning to change under his feet. The familiar, soft earth of Bethlehem gave way to more stone, rougher and more defiant, as if the very ground were flexing its muscles. The scent of dry earth, warm and comforting, now mingled with something else he couldn't quite place – perhaps the fine dust of countless ages, ground down by Time itself, or the sharp, wild tang of unknown rituals carried on the breeze, a scent that prickled the back of his throat.

And then, there it was, a formidable silhouette emerging from the morning haze, growing more distinct with every step. Dan nodded to himself, a prickle of anticipation, perhaps, despite the morning’s growing warmth. The city of Jebus, perched atop its towering cliffs and jagged rock faces like a falcon on its eyrie, seemed almost unreal in his mind – a place of ancient power and whispered secrets, a place where, if one believed the tales, even the very stones might hum with forgotten songs. He had heard tales of its towering temples, their facades grim with age, and the strange, solemn rites that unfolded beneath the watchful, silent gaze of powers long, long forgotten. Powers whose very nature seemed to twist the divine, their hungers not for piety but for a darker sort of obedience, his mind supplied, a chill thought he quickly tried to push away.

As he moved slowly towards the city, its formidable walls and terraces, built high upon the natural rock of the southern slope of a mountain, began to loom into view, asserting their dominance over the landscape. Even before reaching the gates, he began to see unmistakable markers of their faith: carved figures, grotesque and ancient, set into solemn niches along the paths leading up, their faces worn by wind and time; standing stones, rough-hewn and silent, topped with symbols he didn't recognize, symbols that seemed to writhe and coil even in his imagination; small shrines nestled into the rocky slopes, their small altars smudged with dried offerings. These weren't merely decorations; they were declarations, bold pronouncements marking this territory as being under the ever-present, watchful eyes of their mysterious gods. A silent, potent promise to anyone who dared to approach, a warning etched in stone and shadow.

He entered the city proper through a gate, a dark, gaping maw carved directly into the rock face itself, swallowing the morning light. The air inside was different – thicker, warmer, a heavy shroud woven from the cloying, sweet scent of incense, the sharp tang of human sweat, and the acrid, earthy odor of animal waste, a common perfume in any crowded ancient city. The streets were a dizzying ascent, a spiraling ascent carved directly into the very hillside, winding steeply upwards like the coils of a sleeping serpent. Houses were built into terraces, stacked one upon another like precariously balanced wooden blocks, their flat rooftops forming a second, elevated layer of pathways and courtyards, connected by rough-hewn stone steps worn smooth by centuries of hurried feet. The very stone felt alive with history, vibrating with the echoes of countless lives.

Everywhere he looked, within the city walls now, there were more markers of their faith: statues of strange, angular gods carved into niches, standing silently in small squares, their stone eyes following his movements; pillars rising tall, some topped with symbols he didn't recognize, others with familiar crescent moons, dedicated to deities of the night or fertility. The city felt dense, layered, built not just on the hill but of it, a living, breathing monument to its strange, ancient powers.

He needed to find the way towards the highest ground, towards the city gates carved into the rock face high above. He asked a passing townsman, a lean man with weathered hands and a beard flecked with gray, for the direction. The man nodded towards the craggy heights and the distant walls, pointing out crude landmarks — a gnarled cedar tree clinging precariously to the rock, its branches twisted like arthritic fingers; the lazy curve of a rocky stream far below, glinting like a silver ribbon; the jagged outline of a distant tower etched against the brilliant, blue sky.
“Just keep north,” the townsman said with a tired smile, his voice rough as dry leaves, “and the heights will guide you. They say the powers live up there still, watching. Always watching.”

Dan thanked him with a slight bow of his head, and continued on, the path rising steadily beneath his feet. He had been walking alone for a good day, truth be told, and so the observation of the changing landscape was a solitary affair. The hours stretched long, measured only by the sun’s steady crawl across the sky, as he approached the city's highest edge. The soft earth gave way to rocky outcrops, gnarled trees that twisted like ancient dancers, and patches of wild herbs that sent out sharp, clean scents with every rustle of the wind. The air grew colder, carrying a faint saltiness from distant, unseen seas, a whisper of oceans far away.

Occasionally, Dan glimpsed the figures of others on the paths ahead or on the terraces high above — men with heavy cloaks drawn tight against the rising wind, women bearing baskets of fruit or bread, children chasing each other across the rocky ground, their laughter thin and bright in the clear air. Tall, lean, and quick of foot, the Jebusites seemed built on a different scale than those he knew from the southern lands, as if sculpted by the very mountains they inhabited. He had never traveled this far north before, and seeing inhabitants built on such a scale was startling, like stepping into a storybook illustration where the characters had outgrown their pages. There was a certain pride here, a resilience born of the rocky soil and the hard wind that never truly ceased. No one spoke to him, but their eyes held a wary curiosity, a question he couldn’t quite decipher.

As he climbed higher, Dan found his breath coming faster, the cool air filling his lungs with a sharp, invigorating clarity that sharpened his thoughts. The view opened up behind him — the city he had left shrinking into a cluster of tiny rooftops nestled against the southern slope of the mountain, a patchwork quilt of stone and shadow, the golden ribbon of a distant river catching the light far below. And there, far off in the hazy distance, shimmering like a mirage, he could just discern the outlines of the City of Ramah, a whisper of a future destination, a promise on the horizon.

It wasn't that nothing happened in Jebus, for indeed, the world is a stage where countless small dramas play out, even in the quietest corners of existence, particularly within the labyrinth of a young man's thoughts. Rather, it was that no great drama unfolded, no overt event grand enough to halt his journey or demand a redirection of his path. Dan didn't linger. He mostly just walked through, observing, feeling the city's different pulse, noting its powers and its customs. He was a stranger here, a small, curious figure passing through a city built for giants and their stern, watchful gods. And while he moved freely enough, the air itself seemed to carry a subtle chill, a silent message that he was not, truly, welcome to stay.
